source_paper_title: A Primal-Dual Smoothing Framework for Max-Structured Non-Convex Optimization
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Zhao_2024, title={A Primal-Dual Smoothing Framework for Max-Structured Non-Convex Optimization}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.1387}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.1387}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Zhao, Renbo}, year={2024}, month=aug, pages={1535–1565} }
context:
Section 8 (Conclusion and future work), page 35.

First, the lower complexity bound for finding an \(\varepsilon\)-NS point of (1.1) is not known yet. Establishing this bound with dependence on the problem parameters (including \(L_{xx}, L_{xy}, L_{yy}\) and \(\gamma\)) and the accuracy \(\varepsilon\) can be useful to understand the optimality of the existing methods (including ours).
title: Lower bound on oracle complexity for near-stationary max-structured nonconvex optimization
background_latex:
Let \((X,\|\cdot\|_X)\) and \((Y,\|\cdot\|_Y)\) be finite-dimensional real normed spaces. Let \(r:X\to\mathbb{R}\cup\{+\infty\}\) be closed and convex with domain \(\mathrm{dom}r\subseteq X\), and write \(\mathcal{X}:=\mathrm{dom}r\) (assumed nonempty, closed, convex). Let \(\mathcal{Y}\subseteq Y\) be nonempty, closed, convex, and bounded. Let \(g:\mathcal{Y}\to\mathbb{R}\) be convex and continuous.

Let \(\Phi:\mathcal{X}'\times\mathcal{Y}'\to\mathbb{R}\) be jointly continuous on open sets \(\mathcal{X}'\supseteq \mathcal{X}\), \(\mathcal{Y}'\supseteq \mathcal{Y}\), with \(\Phi(x,\cdot)\) concave on \(\mathcal{Y}\) for each \(x\in\mathcal{X}\). Assume \(\Phi(\cdot,y)\) is Frchet differentiable and has Lipschitz gradient in \(x\): there is \(L_{xx}<\infty\) such that for all \(x,x'\in\mathcal{X}\), \(y\in\mathcal{Y}\),
\[\|\nabla_x\Phi(x,y)-\nabla_x\Phi(x',y)\|_{X^*}\le L_{xx}\|x-x'\|_X.\]
Assume also a cross-Lipschitz bound: there is \(L_{xy}<\infty\) such that for all \(x\in\mathcal{X}\), \(y,y'\in\mathcal{Y}\),
\[\|\nabla_x\Phi(x,y)-\nabla_x\Phi(x,y')\|_{X^*}\le L_{xy}\|y-y'\|_Y.\]
Assume weak convexity in \(x\): there exists \(\gamma\in(0,L_{xx}]\) such that for all \(x,x'\in\mathcal{X}\), \(y\in\mathcal{Y}\),
\[\Phi(x',y)\ge \Phi(x,y)+\langle \nabla_x\Phi(x,y),x'-x\rangle-\tfrac{\gamma}{2}\|x'-x\|_X^2.\]
Assume \(\Phi(x,\cdot)\) is Frchet differentiable with Lipschitz gradient in \(y\): there exist \(L_{yy}<\infty\) and the same \(L_{xy}\) such that for all \(x,x'\in\mathcal{X}\), \(y,y'\in\mathcal{Y}\),
\[\|\nabla_y\Phi(x,y)-\nabla_y\Phi(x',y)\|_{Y^*}\le L_{xy}\|x-x'\|_X,\qquad \|\nabla_y\Phi(x,y)-\nabla_y\Phi(x,y')\|_{Y^*}\le L_{yy}\|y-y'\|_Y.\]

Define the max-structured nonconvex objective
\[q(x):=f(x)+r(x),\qquad f(x):=\max_{y\in\mathcal{Y}}\ \Phi(x,y)-g(y),\qquad q^*:=\inf_{x\in\mathcal{X}} q(x)>-\infty.\]

Fix a distance generating function (DGF) \(\omega_X:\mathcal{X}\to\mathbb{R}\) that is 1-strongly convex on \(\mathcal{X}\) and differentiable on a neighborhood of \(\mathcal{X}\), and assume \(\nabla \omega_X\) is \(\beta_X\)-Lipschitz on \(\mathcal{X}\) for some \(\beta_X\ge1\). Let the induced Bregman divergence be
\[D_{\omega_X}(x',x):=\omega_X(x')-\omega_X(x)-\langle \nabla\omega_X(x),x'-x\rangle.\]
For any \(\lambda\in(0,\gamma^{-1})\), define the proximal point mapping
\[\mathrm{prox}(q,x,\lambda):=\arg\min_{x'\in\mathcal{X}}\ q(x')+\lambda^{-1}D_{\omega_X}(x',x).\]
An \(\varepsilon\)-near-stationary point is a point \(x\in\mathcal{X}\) for which there exists \(\lambda\in(0,\gamma^{-1})\) such that
\[\|\lambda^{-1}(x-\mathrm{prox}(q,x,\lambda))\|_X\le \varepsilon/\beta_X.\]

The paper studies first-order methods whose cost is measured by the number of evaluations of \(\nabla_x\Phi(x,y)\) and \(\nabla_y\Phi(x,y)\) (and associated simple Bregman proximal projections for \(r\) and \(g\)), yielding upper bounds on the oracle complexities to obtain an \(\varepsilon\)-near-stationary point. The open question concerns matching information-theoretic (worst-case) lower bounds as functions of \(\varepsilon\) and the parameters \(L_{xx},L_{xy},L_{yy},\gamma\).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine a worst-case lower bound (in terms of \(\varepsilon\) and the problem parameters \(L_{xx},L_{xy},L_{yy},\gamma\), and any necessary dependence on \(\beta_X\)) on the number of first-order oracle calls required by any algorithm to output an \(\varepsilon\)-near-stationary point of
\[\min_{x\in\mathcal{X}}\ q(x)=\max_{y\in\mathcal{Y}}\ \Phi(x,y)-g(y)+r(x),\]
over the class of instances satisfying the smoothness, cross-Lipschitz, and weak-convexity conditions stated in the background. Here a first-order oracle call reveals \(\nabla_x\Phi(x,y)\) and/or \(\nabla_y\Phi(x,y)\) at queried \((x,y)\in\mathcal{X}\times\mathcal{Y}\), along with the ability to compute the required Bregman proximal projections for \(r\) and \(g\).

open_problem_quote: First, the lower complexity bound for finding an \(\varepsilon\)-NS point of (1.1) is not known yet. Establishing this bound with dependence on the problem parameters (including \(L_{xx}, L_{xy}, L_{yy}\) and \(\gamma\)) and the accuracy \(\varepsilon\) can be useful to understand the optimality of the existing methods (including ours).
counterexample_suitability_rationale: Lower bounds typically require adversarial instance constructions and information-based arguments rather than searching for a finite counterexample. While one can numerically test hard instances, that does not certify a universal oracle lower bound.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary code loop could search for hard instance families (specific \(\Phi,g,r\), norms, and DGFs) that empirically maximize the number of oracle calls needed by candidate algorithms, providing conjectures and candidate worst-case constructions. However, the fitness signal (measured complexity) is noisy and algorithm-dependent, and translating discovered instances into rigorous lower-bound proofs remains nontrivial.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
Zhao’s primal-dual smoothing framework targets \(\varepsilon\)-near-stationarity for the composite max-structured weakly convex objective \(q(x)=\max_{y\in\mathcal Y}\Phi(x,y)-g(y)+r(x)\) under separate smoothness and cross-Lipschitz constants \(L_{xx},L_{xy},L_{yy}\), weak convexity \(\gamma\), and non-Euclidean (Bregman) geometry controlled by \(\beta_X\). The algorithmic side is comparatively well developed: Zhao obtains deterministic oracle upper bounds of order \(\tilde O(\varepsilon^{-3})\) (with explicit dependence on \(\gamma,L_{xx},L_{xy},L_{yy},\beta_X\)) for primal/dual gradient evaluations by reducing each outer proximal step to a strongly convex–concave saddle-point problem and solving it via a non-Hilbertian inexact accelerated proximal-gradient scheme.

On the lower-bound side, the forward-citation set shows only partial progress, primarily in more restrictive minimax subclasses. The most directly relevant is “The complexity of nonconvex-strongly-concave minimax optimization,” which proves worst-case first-order oracle lower bounds for finding \(\varepsilon\)-stationary points of the primal max function in smooth nonconvex–strongly-concave minimax problems, with complexity \(\Omega(\sqrt{\kappa\,\Delta L}\,\varepsilon^{-2})\) for deterministic linear-span methods. This establishes that nontrivial parameter-dependent lower bounds are possible and that condition-number effects can be inherent, but it does not cover Zhao’s bounded-\(\mathcal Y\) concave (not strongly concave) setting, composite terms \(r,g\), or Bregman-prox near-stationarity.

Other citing works largely advance upper bounds (e.g., single-loop extragradient variants achieving \(O(\varepsilon^{-2})\) for certain stationarity notions, and proximal/Catalyst frameworks yielding \(\tilde O(\varepsilon^{-3})\) for Moreau-envelope stationarity in nonconvex–concave minimax). These results sharpen the landscape of achievable rates but do not provide matching information-theoretic lower bounds for the full max-structured weakly convex composite class. Consequently, the specific open problem posed by Zhao—deriving worst-case lower bounds with explicit dependence on \(\varepsilon\) and \(L_{xx},L_{xy},L_{yy},\gamma\) (and possibly \(\beta_X\)) for the \(\varepsilon\)-near-stationarity mapping—appears to remain open.

sources.title: The complexity of nonconvex-strongly-concave minimax optimization
sources.theorem_or_result:
For smooth nonconvex–strongly-concave minimax problems \(\min_x\max_y f(x,y)\) with \(f\) jointly \(L\)-smooth and \(\mu\)-strongly concave in \(y\) (condition number \(\kappa=L/\mu\)), the paper proves worst-case first-order oracle lower bounds for finding an \(\varepsilon\)-stationary point of the primal function \(\Phi(x)=\max_y f(x,y)\):
- General (single-function) setting: any deterministic linear-span algorithm using the first-order oracle must make \(\Omega\big(\sqrt{\kappa\,\Delta L}\,\varepsilon^{-2}\big)\) gradient evaluations (Theorem 3.1), for suitable regimes of \(\varepsilon\), where \(\Delta=\Phi(x_0)-\inf_x\Phi(x)\).
- Finite-sum averaged-smooth setting: any (possibly randomized) linear-span algorithm using an incremental first-order oracle must make \(\Omega\big(n+\sqrt{n\kappa\,\Delta L}\,\varepsilon^{-2}\big)\) oracle calls (Theorem 3.2, under \(L\ge 2n\mu\) and small enough \(\varepsilon\)).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for information-theoretic lower bounds on first-order oracle complexity to obtain an \(\varepsilon\)-near-stationary point for max-structured weakly convex objectives \(q(x)=\max_{y\in\mathcal Y}\Phi(x,y)-g(y)+r(x)\) under Lipschitz and cross-Lipschitz assumptions. This paper provides explicit lower bounds for a closely related subclass: unconstrained smooth minimax with \(r\equiv 0\), \(g\equiv 0\), Euclidean geometry, and *strong concavity* in \(y\) (not merely bounded \(\mathcal Y\)). Their lower bound scales as \(\Omega(\sqrt{\kappa\Delta L}\,\varepsilon^{-2})\), exhibiting a nontrivial dependence on the condition number \(\kappa\). However, it does not cover the full generality of the open problem: it does not address general norm/Bregman geometry (\(\beta_X\)), composite terms \(r,g\), bounded \(\mathcal Y\) without strong concavity, nor the weak-convexity parameter \(\gamma\) and separate constants \(L_{xx},L_{xy},L_{yy}\). Thus it constitutes partial progress by establishing lower bounds in an important special case (NC–SC minimax) and offering techniques (zero-chain constructions) potentially adaptable to the broader max-structured setting.

sources.title: A single-loop accelerated extra-gradient difference algorithm with improved complexity bounds for constrained minimax optimization
sources.theorem_or_result: For smooth constrained minimax problems \(\min_{x\in X}\max_{y\in Y} f(x,y)\) with \(X,Y\) closed convex and \(Y\) compact, and \(f\) continuously differentiable with Lipschitz partial gradients (Assumption 1), the proposed single-loop EGDA method outputs an \(\varepsilon\)-stationary point of \(f\) (in the projected-gradient-mapping sense \(\|\pi(x,y)\|\le \varepsilon\)) in \(O((G_0-G+2L D_Y^2)/\varepsilon^2)\) iterations/gradient evaluations (Theorem 1). In the nonconvex–concave case, it outputs an \(\varepsilon\)-stationary point of the primal function \(\phi(x)=\max_{y\in Y} f(x,y)\) measured via the Moreau envelope gradient \(\|\nabla \phi_{1/(2L)}(x)\|\le \varepsilon\) in \(O(D_Y(G_0-G+2L D_Y^2)/\varepsilon^2)\) gradient evaluations (Theorem 2).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for information-theoretic lower bounds on first-order oracle complexity for finding an \(\varepsilon\)-near-stationary point of a max-structured weakly convex objective \(q(x)=\max_{y\in\mathcal Y}\Phi(x,y)-g(y)+r(x)\) under separate smoothness constants \(L_{xx},L_{xy},L_{yy}\) and weak convexity \(\gamma\), with Bregman proximal stationarity. This citing paper provides improved upper bounds (\(O(\varepsilon^{-2})\)) for a related but different model: smooth constrained minimax \(\min_{x\in X}\max_{y\in Y} f(x,y)\) with Euclidean projections and a stationarity notion for \(f\) (or Moreau-envelope stationarity for \(\phi\) in NC-C). It does not establish any worst-case lower bound, nor does it treat the composite terms \(r,g\), Bregman prox geometry, or parameter dependence in \(L_{xx},L_{xy},L_{yy},\gamma,\beta_X\). Thus it is partial progress only insofar as it tightens achievable upper bounds in a neighboring oracle model, suggesting \(\Omega(\varepsilon^{-2})\) may be the right lower-bound order, but it does not prove such a lower bound for the open-problem class.

sources.title: A stochastic smoothing framework for nonconvex-nonconcave min-sum-max problems with applications to wasserstein distributionally robust optimization
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proposes SSPG, a stochastic log-sum-exp smoothing proximal-gradient framework for nonconvex–nonconcave min-sum-max problems \(\min_y \phi(y)+\tfrac1n\sum_{i=1}^n \max_{z\in Z}\Psi(y,z;x_i)\). Under compactness and Lipschitz assumptions, it proves: (i) the primal objective \(g\) is Clarke regular (Lemma 2.3), so Clarke stationarity implies directional stationarity (Corollary 2.1); (ii) the log-sum-exp smoothing \(\Phi_i^e(y,\mu)=\mu\log\mathbb E_{z\sim\zeta}[e^{\Psi(y,z;x_i)/\mu}]\) has \(\nabla_y\Phi_i^e\) Lipschitz with constant \(L_\Psi+2l_\Psi^2/\mu\) (Lemma 2.4); (iii) SSPG outputs an \(\varepsilon\)-scaled Clarke stationary point in expectation in \(\tilde O(\varepsilon^{-3})\) iterations (Corollaries 2.2–2.3), and accumulation points are a.s. directional stationary for the original nonsmooth problem (Theorem 2.1).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for information-theoretic lower bounds on first-order oracle complexity for finding an \(\varepsilon\)-near-stationary point of a max-structured weakly convex objective \(q(x)=\max_{y\in\mathcal Y}\Phi(x,y)-g(y)+r(x)\) under smoothness/cross-Lipschitz/weak-convexity assumptions, with oracle access to \(\nabla_x\Phi\) and \(\nabla_y\Phi\). This citing paper does not provide any lower bound. It gives an upper-bound iteration complexity \(\tilde O(\varepsilon^{-3})\) for a different but related class: min-sum-max problems treated via log-sum-exp smoothing, targeting Clarke/directional stationarity (and an \(\varepsilon\)-scaled stationarity notion) rather than the Bregman-prox near-stationarity mapping used in the open problem. Its techniques (smoothing the max, controlling Lipschitz constants as \(\mu\downarrow0\), and stationarity measures) are conceptually related and could inform constructions for lower bounds, but it does not address the oracle lower-bound question nor match parameter dependence \((L_{xx},L_{xy},L_{yy},\gamma,\beta_X)\).

sources.title: Efficient search of first-order nash equilibria in nonconvex-concave smooth min-max problems
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper gives a first-order method for smooth nonconvex–concave min–max problems \(\min_{x\in X}\max_{y\in Y} F(x,y)\) (with Lipschitz constants \(L_{xx},L_{xy},L_{yy}\), compact \(Y\)) that outputs an \((\varepsilon_x,\varepsilon_y)\)-first-order Nash equilibrium (FNE) under a strong constrained stationarity measure \(S_Z\) in \(\widetilde O(T_x T_y)\) gradient/projection calls, where \(T_x=\tfrac{L_{xx}\Delta}{\varepsilon_x^2}\) and \(T_y=\sqrt{\tfrac{(L_{yy}+L_{xy}^2/L_{xx})R_y}{\varepsilon_y}}\). In particular, choosing \(\varepsilon_y=\Theta(\varepsilon_x^2/(L_{xx}R_y))\) yields \(\widetilde O(\varepsilon_x^{-3})\) complexity for finding an \(\varepsilon_x\)-stationary point of the standard Moreau envelope of the primal function \(\varphi(x)=\max_{y\in Y}F(x,y)\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for information-theoretic lower bounds on first-order oracle complexity for finding an \(\varepsilon\)-near-stationary point of a max-structured weakly nonconvex objective \(q(x)=\max_{y\in\mathcal Y}\Phi(x,y)-g(y)+r(x)\) under smoothness/cross-Lipschitz/weak-convexity assumptions. This citing paper provides improved upper bounds (algorithms) for the special case \(r\equiv 0\), \(g\equiv 0\), Euclidean geometry, and concave (not merely general max-structured) dependence in \(y\). It does not prove any worst-case lower bound, nor does it address the full composite/Bregman framework of the open problem. However, its rates \(\widetilde O(\varepsilon_x^{-2}\varepsilon_y^{-1/2})\) and the induced \(\widetilde O(\varepsilon^{-3})\) Moreau-envelope stationarity rate are natural candidates to be matched by lower bounds, and it discusses (citing Carmon–Duchi–Hinder–Sidford) known lower bounds for smooth nonconvex minimization that may inform the max-structured setting.

sources.title: Near-optimal algorithms for minimax optimization
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper gives near-optimal first-order upper bounds for smooth minimax problems. In particular, for `-smooth, \(\mu_x\)-strongly-convex–\(\mu_y\)-strongly-concave \(f\), it proves an algorithm (MINIMAX-APPA) that outputs an \(\varepsilon\)-saddle point using \(\tilde O(\sqrt{\kappa_x\kappa_y})\) gradient evaluations, where \(\kappa_x=\ell/\mu_x\), \(\kappa_y=\ell/\mu_y\) (Theorem 9). For nonconvex–(strongly)-concave settings it proves upper bounds such as \(\tilde O(\sqrt{\kappa_y}\,\varepsilon^{-2})\) to find an \(\varepsilon\)-stationary point of \(f\) (Theorem 12) and \(\tilde O(\varepsilon^{-3})\) for stationarity of \(\Phi(x)=\max_y f(x,y)\) in the concave (not strongly concave) case (Corollary 21).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for information-theoretic lower bounds on oracle complexity for finding an \(\varepsilon\)-near-stationary point of a max-structured weakly convex objective \(q(x)=\max_{y\in\mathcal Y}\Phi(x,y)-g(y)+r(x)\) under Lipschitz/cross-Lipschitz and weak-convexity assumptions, with an oracle returning \(\nabla_x\Phi,\nabla_y\Phi\). This citing paper does not prove lower bounds for this class. It focuses on designing (near-)optimal algorithms and matching known lower bounds in the strongly-convex–strongly-concave minimax setting, and it explicitly states that lower bounds for nonconvex-(strongly)-concave minimax remain open. Thus it provides context and upper-bound techniques (proximal point + accelerated inner minimax solver) but does not resolve the lower-bound question posed in the max-structured nonconvex framework.

sources.title: A catalyst framework for minimax optimization
sources.theorem_or_result:
For smooth minimax problems with concavity in \(y\) and either strong convexity in \(x\) (SC-C) or nonconvexity in \(x\) (NC-C), the paper proposes a Catalyst (accelerated proximal point) framework that yields oracle complexities:
- SC-C: with extragradient/OGDA as inner solver and \(\tau=\mu\), total gradient evaluations \(\tilde O\big( \ell D_Y/(\sqrt{\mu\,\varepsilon})\,\log(\ell D_Y/(\min\{1,\mu\}\varepsilon))\big)\) to reach primal-dual gap \(\le \varepsilon\).
- NC-C: choosing \(\tau_x=2\ell\), \(\tau_y=\ell\), total gradient evaluations \(\tilde O(\ell^2\,\varepsilon^{-3})\) to find \(\bar x\) with \(\mathbb E\|\nabla \psi_{1/\tau_x}(\bar x)\|\le \varepsilon\) (stationarity via Moreau envelope).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for information-theoretic lower bounds (in \(\varepsilon\) and parameters \(L_{xx},L_{xy},L_{yy},\gamma,\beta_X\)) for first-order oracle complexity to find an \(\varepsilon\)-near-stationary point of a max-structured weakly convex objective \(q(x)=\max_y \Phi(x,y)-g(y)+r(x)\) under cross-Lipschitz and smoothness assumptions.

This citing paper is primarily algorithmic and focuses on smooth minimax \(\min_x\max_y f(x,y)\) with Euclidean norms, no nonsmooth \(r,g\), and stationarity measured by the Moreau-envelope gradient of \(g(x)=\max_y f(x,y)\). It cites lower bounds for SC-C duality-gap complexity (e.g., \(\Omega(\ell/(\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}))\) from prior work) but does not derive new lower bounds for the weakly convex max-structured composite setting of the open problem. At best, it provides upper bounds/state-of-the-art rates in related NC-C settings, which may inform what a matching lower bound might look like (e.g., \(\varepsilon^{-3}\) dependence), but it does not address the requested parameterized lower bound in the general framework (including \(L_{xy},L_{yy},\gamma,\beta_X\) and Bregman geometry).
